title: Shipwrecks of Shackleton and Scott recreated in 3D digital form after deep sea expedition
content: # Shipwrecks of Shackleton and Scott recreated in 3D digital form after deep sea expedition **LOPINUZE —** A team of Canadian marine scientists has successfully completed a groundbreaking deep-sea expedition to create ultra-high-resolution 3D digital replicas of two of history's most iconic polar exploration vessels: the *Quest*, the final ship of Sir Ernest Shackleton, and the *Terra Nova*, the vessel that carried Captain Robert Falcon Scott on his ill-fated Antarctic expedition. The project, described by researchers as a "golden era for shipwreck investigating," has yielded photorealistic digital twins of the wrecks, which lie more than 1,000 feet (305 meters) below the surface of the Labrador Sea, off the coast of Canada. ## Expedition reveals 'skeleton' of Shackleton's final ship Moments after devouring the final glimmers of light, the seafloor offered nothing but darkness and silt. Then the bow appeared. More than 1,000ft below the surface, the skeleton of the *Quest*, the vessel used by famed polar explorer Ernest Shackleton on his last voyage in 1921, emerged from its silty grave. The expedition, led by the Royal Canadian Geographical Society and Ocean Infinity, deployed autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) equipped with side-scan sonar and 4K cameras to capture every rivet and plank of the wreck. "Seeing the *Quest* in such pristine condition, after nearly 100 years on the seafloor, was an emotional moment for the entire team," said Dr. Marie-Claude Thériault, chief marine archaeologist for the expedition. "The wreck is a time capsule, perfectly preserved by the cold, dark waters. We can now measure its length to within centimeters and identify structural damage that may have contributed to its sinking." The *Quest* sank in 1962 after striking ice, decades after Shackleton's death aboard the same ship in 1922. The 3D model, built from over 2,500 individual photographs, reveals the vessel's hull intact, with its wheelhouse and masts still standing. ## Scott's *Terra Nova* yields new data on sinking The expedition also revisited the wreck of the *Terra Nova*, which sank in 1943 after being damaged by ice and scuttled by the Royal Canadian Navy. The ship, best known for carrying Scott's 1910–1913 Antarctic expedition, lies just 12 nautical miles from the *Quest*. The new 3D model shows the *Terra Nova* resting on its starboard side, with its bow partially buried in sediment. Data from the survey suggests the ship's final moments were more violent than previously thought, with evidence of a hull breach on the port side. "This is a golden era for shipwreck investigating," said Dr. James MacInnis, a senior historian with the Canadian Museum of History. "We are no longer just looking at grainy sonar images. We can walk through these wrecks digitally, measure every angle, and share them with the world in a way that was impossible even a decade ago." ## How digital twins are transforming maritime archaeology The 3D models were created using a technique called photogrammetry, where thousands of overlapping images are stitched together by software to create a precise, textured digital replica. The models are accurate to within 5 millimeters, allowing researchers to study corrosion patterns, structural stress, and even the growth of marine life on the wrecks. The data is being shared with the Science Desk and marine heritage organizations globally. "The *Quest* and *Terra Nova* are not just shipwrecks; they are monuments to human endurance and tragedy," said Dr. Thériault. "By creating these digital twins, we are ensuring that their stories will survive for future generations, even if the physical wrecks eventually decay." ## Forward-looking analysis: The future of deep-sea heritage This expedition marks a significant leap in underwater archaeology. As climate change accelerates ice melt and ocean acidification, many historic wrecks face unprecedented threats. The ability to create high-fidelity digital twins not only preserves these sites virtually but also provides baseline data for monitoring degradation. The team plans to release the 3D models to the public via an interactive online platform later this year, allowing anyone with an internet connection to explore the wrecks from their own home. The success of the mission also opens the door for further exploration of other deep-sea wrecks, including those from World War II and earlier polar expeditions. As technology improves, the line between physical exploration and digital preservation continues to blur, promising a new era of discovery for historians and the public alike.
